subject: Equity Law
lesson_title: EVERY WHICH WAY BUT LOOSE — THE MODES OF CONSTITUTION

questions.question: What does 'constitution' of a trust mean in trust law?

options.A: The drafting and execution of a formal trust deed
options.B: The process by which a trust becomes fully effective — legally complete and binding
options.C: The registration of the trust with the relevant government authority
options.D: The appointment of at least two trustees to manage the trust property

questions.explanation: The 'constitution' of a trust refers to the process by which a trust becomes fully effective — legally complete and binding. Until a trust is properly constituted, it may be unenforceable, particularly by a volunteer. Constitution requires that legal title to the trust property reaches the trustee in the correct manner.

questions.question: The equitable maxim 'equity will not assist a volunteer' means that:

options.A: Charitable trusts cannot be enforced without the involvement of the Attorney General
options.B: If a settlor promises to create a trust but never completes it, equity will not compel the transfer on behalf of a beneficiary who gave no consideration
options.C: A trustee who acts without payment cannot be held liable for breach of trust
options.D: Equity will not grant injunctions in cases where no money is at stake

questions.explanation: 'Equity will not assist a volunteer' means that if a settlor promises to create a trust but never actually completes it, equity will not step in to compel the transfer of property to the trustee on behalf of a beneficiary who has not given consideration. The promise remains incomplete and unenforceable. This was firmly established in Milroy v Lord [1862].

questions.question: In Milroy v Lord [1862], Turner LJ laid down how many modes by which a trust can be constituted?

options.A: Two — a gift or a self-declaration of trust
options.B: Three — transfer on trust, outright gift, or self-declaration of trust
options.C: Four — transfer, gift, declaration, or donatio mortis causa
options.D: Two — transfer on trust or direction to trustees

questions.explanation: In Milroy v Lord [1862], Turner LJ laid down three modes by which a voluntary settlement can be valid and effectual: (1) transfer of trust property to trustees for the benefit of beneficiaries (transfer on trust); (2) outright transfer of property to the intended beneficiary (gift); (3) declaration by the settlor that they hold the property as trustee (self-declaration of trust).

questions.question: The principle stated by Turner LJ in Milroy v Lord [1862] that 'there is no equity to perfect an imperfect gift' means:

options.A: An imperfect gift can always be perfected by an oral undertaking from the donor
options.B: If the settlor has not completed the transfer by the correct legal method, equity will not intervene to complete it
options.C: A gift can only be perfect if it is made by deed
options.D: The beneficiary of an imperfect gift can sue in tort to recover the property

questions.explanation: 'There is no equity to perfect an imperfect gift' means that if the settlor has not done one of the three things perfectly — transfer, gift, or self-declaration — the trust or gift fails. Equity will not intervene to complete what the settlor has left incomplete. This is the central rule of the constitution of trusts.

questions.question: For a transfer on trust of shares in a company to be effective, what steps must be taken?

options.A: Delivery of the share certificates to the trustee
options.B: Execution of a proper share transfer form and registration by the company
options.C: Oral notification to the company secretary of the intended trust
options.D: A deed of gift executed before a notary public

questions.explanation: For a transfer on trust of shares in a company to be effective, the correct legal formalities must be followed: execution of a proper share transfer form and registration of the transfer by the company. Until both steps are completed, legal title has not passed and the trust is not constituted. This was the issue at the heart of Re Rose [1952].

questions.question: The Re Rose principle [1952] holds that a transfer of shares is complete in equity when:

options.A: The company registers the new shareholding in its register
options.B: The transferor has done everything required of them — even if a third party (the company) has not yet completed registration
options.C: The share certificates are delivered to the trustee
options.D: Both the transferor and transferee have signed the share transfer form

questions.explanation: The Re Rose principle holds that a transfer is complete in equity when the transferor has done everything required of them — even if a third party such as a company has not yet completed registration. In Re Rose [1952], the settlor had executed share transfer forms and delivered them to the transferee, and the Court of Appeal held the transfer was complete in equity from that moment.

questions.question: In a self-declaration of trust, the settlor:

options.A: Transfers legal title of property to a separate, independent trustee
options.B: Declares that they themselves hold their own property on trust for another, without any change of legal title
options.C: Transfers both legal and beneficial title to the trustee simultaneously
options.D: Appoints a beneficiary to become a co-trustee alongside the settlor

questions.explanation: In a self-declaration of trust, the settlor declares that they hold their own property on trust for another person. There is no transfer of legal title — the settlor becomes the trustee. The trust is constituted the moment the declaration is made because the legal owner already holds the property and has simply declared themselves a trustee.

questions.question: Why are courts reluctant to infer that a person has declared themselves a trustee?

options.A: Because self-declarations of trust are not recognised in Irish law
options.B: Because declaring oneself a trustee imposes onerous fiduciary obligations
options.C: Because the Statute of Frauds requires all self-declarations to be in writing
options.D: Because courts prefer outright gifts to the creation of trusts

questions.explanation: Courts are reluctant to infer that a person has declared themselves a trustee because doing so imposes onerous fiduciary obligations on that person. The declaration must be clear and genuine. Trusteeship is a heavy burden and courts will not lightly treat ambiguous statements as amounting to a self-declaration of trust.

questions.question: In Jones v Lock [1865], a father put a cheque in his infant son's hand saying 'I give this to baby'. The court held:

options.A: The words constituted a valid self-declaration of trust
options.B: The words were not sufficient to declare the father a trustee — it was an imperfect gift
options.C: The gift was valid because delivery of the cheque to the infant completed it
options.D: The trust was valid as a donatio mortis causa

questions.explanation: In Jones v Lock [1865], the court held that the father's words were not sufficient to declare himself a trustee — it was an imperfect gift. The father had intended to make a gift, not declare himself a trustee. Equity would not treat a failed gift as a declaration of trust merely to save the transaction. The father had not used words sufficient to declare himself a trustee.

questions.question: Richards v Delbridge [1874] confirmed that equity will NOT:

options.A: Treat a failed gift as a declaration of trust merely to save the transaction
options.B: Enforce a trust where the three certainties are not satisfied
options.C: Recognise trusts of future property created by a volunteer
options.D: Allow a trustee to make a profit from their position

questions.explanation: Richards v Delbridge [1874] confirmed that equity will not treat an imperfect gift as a declaration of trust merely to save the transaction. Jessel MR held that where the donor's intention was to give and not to declare a trust, equity respects that distinction and will not impose a trust to perfect a failed gift. The donor must have intended to create a trust, not a gift.

questions.question: For a transfer on trust of a chattel (physical personal property) to be effective, which of the following is required?

options.A: Registration with the Land Registry
options.B: Delivery of the chattel or execution of a deed of gift
options.C: A written trust instrument signed by the settlor and trustee
options.D: Filing of a notice of charge with the Companies Registration Office

questions.explanation: For a transfer on trust of a chattel (physical personal property) to be effective, delivery of the chattel or execution of a deed of gift is required. Different types of property require different formalities: land requires a deed and registration; shares require a transfer form and registration; and chattels require delivery or a deed.

questions.question: The rule in Milroy v Lord requires that the correct mode of transfer be used for each type of property. Which formality applies to a transfer on trust of registered land?

options.A: Execution of share transfer forms
options.B: Physical delivery of the title deeds to the trustee
options.C: Deed of conveyance and registration in the Land Registry
options.D: Written declaration signed by the transferor before a solicitor

questions.explanation: A transfer on trust of registered land requires a deed of conveyance and registration in the Land Registry. Each type of property has its own required mode of transfer: shares require a share transfer form and company registration; chattels require delivery or a deed; and registered land requires a deed and registration.

questions.question: Where a settlor has done 'everything in their power' to effect a transfer but a third party has not yet completed its part, equity treats the transfer as:

options.A: Incomplete and unenforceable until the third party acts
options.B: Complete in equity from the moment the settlor completed their part
options.C: Void for non-compliance with legal formalities
options.D: A resulting trust in favour of the settlor until the third party acts

questions.explanation: Under the Re Rose principle [1952], where a settlor has done everything in their power to effect a transfer — and the only remaining step is for a third party such as a company to complete registration — equity treats the transfer as complete from the moment the settlor completed their part. This is the 'Re Rose principle' — the transfer is complete in equity when the transferor has done all that lies within their power.

questions.question: Which of the following is a volunteer for the purposes of the rule 'equity will not assist a volunteer'?

options.A: A beneficiary who paid consideration for the creation of the trust
options.B: A beneficiary under a marriage settlement who is within the marriage consideration
options.C: A beneficiary named in a voluntary trust who has given no consideration
options.D: The trustee who received property by way of legal transfer

questions.explanation: A volunteer is a person who has not provided consideration for the creation of the trust or the transfer of property. A beneficiary named in a voluntary trust who has given no consideration is a volunteer. Equity will not assist such a person to enforce an incompletely constituted trust — the promise of the trust remains unenforceable by them.

questions.question: If a settlor attempts to transfer shares by writing 'I give my 500 XYZ shares to my sister as trustee' on the back of an envelope and hands it to her, without executing a proper transfer form, the trust is:

options.A: Fully constituted because the settlor expressed clear intention and handed the document to the trustee
options.B: Constituted in equity under the Re Rose principle
options.C: Not constituted because the correct legal formalities for transferring shares have not been followed
options.D: Constituted because a self-declaration of trust requires no formal transfer

questions.explanation: The trust is not constituted because the correct legal formalities for transferring shares — execution of a proper share transfer form and company registration — have not been followed. Writing on an envelope is not the correct method. Under Milroy v Lord, if the settlor has not done everything required by the correct method, equity will not perfect the imperfect transfer.

questions.question: Following Milroy v Lord, which principle applies where a donor attempts to make a gift by transfer but fails to complete the correct formalities?

options.A: The gift is treated as a self-declaration of trust in favour of the intended recipient
options.B: The gift fails and there is no equity to perfect it
options.C: The gift is valid at common law even if it fails in equity
options.D: The intended recipient can compel completion by seeking specific performance

questions.explanation: Following Milroy v Lord, where a donor attempts to make a gift by transfer but fails to complete the correct formalities, the gift fails — and 'there is no equity to perfect an imperfect gift'. The donor's failure to follow the correct mode of transfer means the gift is incomplete, and equity will not step in to complete what the donor left unfinished.

questions.question: The term 'volunteer' in the context of constitution of trusts is BEST defined as:

options.A: A person who acts as trustee without payment
options.B: A person who has not given consideration for the trust or transfer
options.C: A person who voluntarily discloses a trust to the Revenue Commissioners
options.D: A settlor who creates a trust without legal advice

questions.explanation: In the context of constitution of trusts, a 'volunteer' is a person who has not given consideration for the trust or transfer. The maxim 'equity will not assist a volunteer' means that a beneficiary who has not provided consideration cannot compel equity to perfect an incompletely constituted trust in their favour.

questions.question: In the context of constitution, what is the significance of the Re Rose principle for transfer on trust?

options.A: It extends the time limit for completing share registrations to 28 days
options.B: It holds that where the transferor has done everything within their power, equity treats the transfer as complete even before third-party steps are taken
options.C: It holds that a self-declaration of trust can substitute for a formal transfer of shares
options.D: It abolishes the need for company registration in share transfers where there is clear trust intent

questions.explanation: The Re Rose principle [1952] holds that where the transferor has done everything within their power — for example, signed and delivered a share transfer form — equity treats the transfer as complete from that moment, even before the company registers the transfer. This is a significant exception to the strict application of Milroy v Lord.

questions.question: Which of the following CORRECTLY identifies when a self-declaration of trust takes effect?

options.A: When the trust is registered with the appropriate regulatory body
options.B: When the settlor transfers legal title of the property to a trustee
options.C: The moment the clear declaration is made by the property owner
options.D: When the beneficiary receives written notice of the trust

questions.explanation: A self-declaration of trust takes effect the moment the clear declaration is made by the property owner. Since the settlor already holds the legal title, no transfer is required. The trust is constituted immediately upon declaration. The declaration must be clear — courts will not lightly infer a self-declaration due to the onerous fiduciary obligations it imposes.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes why 'equity will not treat a failed gift as a declaration of trust'?

options.A: Because there is no legal distinction between a gift and a trust declaration
options.B: Because the donor's intention was to give property, not to create a fiduciary relationship — equity respects the donor's actual intention
options.C: Because courts refuse to impose obligations on unwilling donors after the failed gift
options.D: Because the three certainties cannot be satisfied if the donor intended a gift

questions.explanation: Equity will not treat a failed gift as a declaration of trust because the donor's intention was to give property outright, not to create a fiduciary relationship as trustee. Equity respects the donor's actual intention — as confirmed in Richards v Delbridge [1874]. Imposing a trust where none was intended would distort the donor's intention and misuse the trust mechanism.

questions.question: Which statement about the three modes of constitution in Milroy v Lord is CORRECT?

options.A: All three modes can be combined in the same transaction to ensure constitution
options.B: If one mode is attempted but fails, the court may convert it into another mode automatically
options.C: Each mode is distinct — the correct mode for each type of property must be used perfectly, and equity will not substitute one mode for another
options.D: The three modes only apply to trusts of land; for personal property, constitution is automatic

questions.explanation: The three modes in Milroy v Lord are distinct — the correct mode for each type of property must be used perfectly. Equity will not substitute one mode for another; an attempt at a transfer that fails cannot be saved by treating it as a self-declaration of trust (Richards v Delbridge). Each mode must be completed correctly in accordance with the formalities applicable to the type of property involved.

questions.question: A settlor declares a trust for the benefit of a volunteer beneficiary but never transfers the property to the trustee. The beneficiary seeks to enforce the trust. Which principle governs?

options.A: The three certainties — if all three are met the trust is enforceable regardless of constitution
options.B: Equity will not assist a volunteer — the incompletely constituted trust cannot be enforced
options.C: The Re Rose principle — equity treats the trust as complete because the settlor expressed intent
options.D: The doctrine of satisfaction — the trust obligation is treated as discharged by the declaration alone

questions.explanation: Where a trust has not been properly constituted — the property has never been transferred to the trustee — and the beneficiary is a volunteer who gave no consideration, the maxim 'equity will not assist a volunteer' applies. The incomplete trust cannot be enforced against the settlor. Mere intent to create a trust, without completion of the correct legal transfer, is insufficient.
